article.title: FDA Approves IMCIVREE as First Treatment for Acquired Hypothalamic Obesity, a Rare Condition With No Prior Therapy
article.summary: Rhythm Pharmaceuticals' setmelanotide won FDA approval on March 19 for acquired hypothalamic obesity after a Phase 3 trial showed an 18.4 percent placebo-adjusted BMI reduction at 52 weeks, giving an estimated 10,000 U.S. patients their first approved treatment option.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

The U.S. Food and Drug Administration approved an expanded indication for IMCIVREE (setmelanotide) on March 19, 2026, making it the first and only FDA-approved therapy for acquired hypothalamic obesity, a rare condition that causes severe and unrelenting weight gain following injury to the hypothalamus. The approval covers adults and pediatric patients aged four years and older, according to [Rhythm Pharmaceuticals' announcement](https://www.globenewswire.com/news-release/2026/03/19/3259502/0/en/Rhythm-Pharmaceuticals-Announces-FDA-Approval-of-IMCIVREE-setmelanotide-for-Patients-with-Acquired-Hypothalamic-Obesity.html).

Acquired hypothalamic obesity affects an estimated 10,000 people in the United States and most commonly develops within six months after treatment for craniopharyngioma, astrocytoma, or other tumors near the hypothalamus. Traumatic brain injury, stroke, and inflammation can also trigger the condition. Until now, patients had no approved pharmacological treatment.

## TRANSCEND Trial Results

The approval was supported by the pivotal Phase 3 TRANSCEND trial, which enrolled 142 patients globally with acquired hypothalamic obesity. The trial met its primary endpoint with a statistically significant placebo-adjusted BMI reduction of 18.4 percent at 52 weeks, as reported by [Fierce Pharma](https://www.fiercepharma.com/pharma/rhythm-changes-its-genetic-obesity-tune-imcivrees-transformative-label-expansion-brain).

Participants receiving setmelanotide (n=94) achieved a 15.8 percent reduction in BMI from baseline, while those on placebo (n=48) experienced a 2.6 percent increase over the same period (p<0.0001). The trial also included 12 Japanese patients and 10 supplemental patients with acquired hypothalamic obesity, bringing the total analysis population to 142, according to [Rhythm Pharmaceuticals](https://www.globenewswire.com/news-release/2026/03/19/3259502/0/en/Rhythm-Pharmaceuticals-Announces-FDA-Approval-of-IMCIVREE-setmelanotide-for-Patients-with-Acquired-Hypothalamic-Obesity.html). Beyond BMI, patients treated with setmelanotide reported clinically meaningful reductions in hunger levels.

## How Setmelanotide Works

Acquired hypothalamic obesity arises because damage to the hypothalamus reduces production of alpha-melanocyte-stimulating hormone, impairing the melanocortin-4 receptor (MC4R) pathway that regulates hunger, energy expenditure, and weight homeostasis. Setmelanotide is an MC4R agonist that directly activates this pathway, addressing the root biological dysfunction rather than treating symptoms.

The drug was previously approved in the United States for patients aged two years and older with Bardet-Biedl syndrome and with obesity caused by POMC, PCSK1, or LEPR deficiency. It also holds approvals in the European Union and the United Kingdom for syndromic obesity conditions. The acquired hypothalamic obesity indication represents a significant expansion into a broader patient population.

## Safety Profile

The most common adverse events reported in the TRANSCEND trial, each occurring in more than 20 percent of participants, were skin hyperpigmentation, nausea, vomiting, and headache, according to [Fierce Pharma](https://www.fiercepharma.com/pharma/rhythm-changes-its-genetic-obesity-tune-imcivrees-transformative-label-expansion-brain). The safety profile was consistent with setmelanotide's established record in other patient populations.

The prescribing information includes several notable warnings. Acute adrenal insufficiency was observed in 5 percent of acquired hypothalamic obesity patients receiving setmelanotide compared with 0 percent on placebo. Hyponatremia occurred in 6 percent of patients with concurrent central diabetes insipidus versus 2 percent on placebo. The label also warns of risk for depression and suicidal ideation, spontaneous penile erections, and serious hypersensitivity reactions.

## Significance

David Meeker, chairman and chief executive of Rhythm Pharmaceuticals, stated that IMCIVREE "is now the first and only FDA-approved therapy for acquired HO, offering a targeted approach that addresses the underlying biology of this disease," according to [Rhythm's announcement](https://www.globenewswire.com/news-release/2026/03/19/3259502/0/en/Rhythm-Pharmaceuticals-Announces-FDA-Approval-of-IMCIVREE-setmelanotide-for-Patients-with-Acquired-Hypothalamic-Obesity.html).

Ashley Shoemaker, a physician at Vanderbilt Health involved in the TRANSCEND trial, noted that "setmelanotide has shown effectiveness in targeting the underlying biology of acquired HO" with meaningful BMI reductions, as reported by [Rhythm Pharmaceuticals](https://www.globenewswire.com/news-release/2026/03/19/3259502/0/en/Rhythm-Pharmaceuticals-Announces-FDA-Approval-of-IMCIVREE-setmelanotide-for-Patients-with-Acquired-Hypothalamic-Obesity.html).

The approval addresses what patient advocates have described as a long-neglected condition. Amy Wood of the Raymond A. Wood Foundation said that families affected by acquired hypothalamic obesity face "relentless hunger" and that prior treatment options were limited to lifestyle interventions and off-label medications with modest efficacy.

While the estimated U.S. patient population of 10,000 is small compared with the broader obesity market, the approval establishes IMCIVREE as the sole targeted therapy for a condition that previously had no pharmacological standard of care. Whether the drug's MC4R mechanism could eventually find application in more common forms of hypothalamic-related weight gain remains an open question for future research.
